rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE CHUNG HOP WONG AND KING TSUI LAU WONG REVOCABL TRUST dated sep 21, 2009 amended Sep 26 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Deeming Admitted Truth Of Facts And Imposing Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Michael McNaughton. Before the court is Petitioners Helen Wong-Joe, Jessica Wong, and Brandon Wong's Motion for Order Deeming Admitted Truth of Facts and Imposing Monetary Sanctions ("Motion"). The Motion's request, per the Notice of Motion, for an order that the Request for Admissions, Set Two served on October 17, 2025 be deemed admitted and conclusively established for all purposes in this action is DENIED. The Motion's request, per the Notice of Motion, for an order imposing sanctions on the responding party for the fees and costs incurred in bringing this motion is GRANTED, in part. I. Motion to Deem Truth of the Matter Admitted Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280, If a party to whom requests for admission are directed fails to serve a timely response [ ] (b) The requesting party may move for an order that the genuineness of any documents and the truth of any matters specified in the requests be deemed admitted, as well as for a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010). (c) The court shall make this order, unless it finds that the party to whom the requests for admission have been directed has served, before the hearing on the motion, a proposed response to the requests for admission that is in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220. [] It is not disputed that the RFAs at issue were served electronically on October 17, 2025. Per Declaration of Daniel C. Hales In Support Of Kathryn Wong's Opposition To Motion To Deem Admitted, counsel for Kathryn Wong, the responding party, did not realize these RFAs had been served, because his secretary at the time "accidentally and mistakenly" deleted the email and the response deadline was not calendared (Hales Decl. at par. 2.) Per the Reply to the Opposition, the responding party served responses on January 7, 2026, prior to the hearing on the Motion. Per the Opposition, these responses "have been served in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure [section] 2033.220." (Opp. at 2:8-9.) There is no statutory meet and confer required prior to filing a motion under Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280, when "a party to whom requests for admission are directed fails to serve a timely response." The parties do not dispute that responses to the RFAs, specifically the response to RFA #73 and #57, are untimely. In the Reply to the Opposition, the movants argue the late responses, specifically the response to RFA #73 and #57, are not "in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220," and thus should still be deemed admitted pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280(b). Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.220 states the requirements for answers to requests for admission: (a) Each answer in a response to requests for admission shall be as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits. (b) Each answer shall: (1) Admit so much of the matter involved in the request as is true, either as expressed in the request itself or as reasonably and clearly qualified by the responding party. (2) Deny so much of the matter involved in the request as is untrue. (3) Specify so much of the matter involved in the request as to the truth of which the responding party lacks sufficient information or knowledge. The case law advises flexibility in determining what qualifies as code-compliant responses, even when the late-responding party is asserting waived objections. "Compliance analysis should prioritize the nature of the substantive answers in the proposed response. While waived objections should be a factor in the assessment, their presence should primarily be addressed through the amount of mandatory monetary sanctions imposed." (See, Katayama v. Cont'l Inv. Grp. (2024) 105 Cal. App. 5th 898, 910.)

rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must do so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE CHUNG HOP WONG AND KING TSUI LAU WONG REVOCABL TRUST dated sep 21, 2009 amended Sep 26 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purspose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The Reply recites only the responses to RFAs #73 and #57. As to RFA #73 the Response was "Denied." This is a complete response. As to RFA #57 the Response was "Admitted." Not only is this a complete response, it is the same response that Movants are seeking to be deemed established. As such, the request is now moot. In either case, both are substantially compliant responses. As to the Response to RFA #57, Movants argue that the admission is contradicted by the responding parties' prior discovery responses. Whether true or not, that issue is not before this court. The motion seeks that the truth of RFA #57 be deemed admitted. Respondent has now admitted it. As to the response to RFA #73, Movants' complaint in the Reply appears to be that Respondent's corresponding response to Form Interrogatory 17.1 as it relates to RFA #73 is inadequate. That issue is not before the court. Movants offer no authority to support the argument that responses to RFAs must also comply with the requirements for responses to Form Interrogatories. The court does not adopt the argument. As to whether the responses are otherwise Code-compliant, the case law on motions to deem admitted brought under section 2033.280 does not discuss whether "in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220" must be synonymous with "objectively true" or even "what the propounding party thinks is true." If, having received the substantially compliant responses, the propounding party deems that an answer to a particular request is evasive or incomplete, and/or an objection to a particular request is without merit or too general, the remedy is to bring a motion to compel further responses under Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.290. As discussed in St. Mary v. Superior Ct. (2014) 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, 783-84, it would be an abuse of discretion for the court to misapply a section 2033.280 motion to deem admitted, to effectively grant a motion to compel further responses under section 2033.290 that has not been filed. II. Sanctions Under Code of Civil Proceeding section 2033.280(c), "It is mandatory that the court impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) on the party or attorney, or both, whose failure to serve a timely response to requests for admission necessitated this motion." The moving party has requested an award of sanctions in the amount of $2,400, based on the declaration of Mr. Malachowski. The Declaration, however, gives no details as to how or why this $2,400 was incurred. The court does wish to highlight to both parties and their attorneys that, although a formal meet and confer process is not required before filing a motion pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280, if counsel for the Movants had informally contacted counsel for the responding party when the deadline for responses had passed and asked about their status, this Motion and the associated expense for both parties may have been rendered unnecessary and discovery in this matter would not have been further delayed by several months. Thus, the Court does not find that the entire $2,400 in fees was "necessarily" incurred. However, the responses were untimely, requiring Movants to take some steps, and incur some fees, in obtaining compliance. Accordingly, the court orders that Daniel C. Hales, the attorney of record for responding party Kathryn Wong, is ordered to pay Petitioners Helen Wong-Joe, Jessica Wong, and Brandon Wong sanctions in the amount of $900 pursuant to Code of Civil Proceeding section 2033.280(c), which it deems to be a reasonable amount of fees incurred due to counsel's failure to provide timely responses.
